intro_text: Hi there! 😊 Today we calculate Surface Areas and Volumes of 3D shapes — cuboids, cylinders, cones, spheres! These formulas are used in engineering, architecture, cooking and packaging. Let's master them! 🎯
complete_message: You have mastered Surface Areas and Volumes! You can now calculate any 3D shape in the real world 💪

explain_questions.question: What are Surface Area and Volume? What is the difference?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are Surface Area and Volume? What is the difference?

steps.t: SURFACE AREA is the total area of ALL the outer faces of a 3D shape — like the amount of wrapping paper needed to cover a gift box. Measured in cm² or m².
steps.s: Surface area is the total outer area of a 3D shape. Think of wrapping paper. Measured in square units.

steps.t: VOLUME is the amount of space inside a 3D shape — like how much water a bottle can hold. Measured in cm³ or m³ (cubic units). Volume = capacity!
steps.s: Volume is the space inside. Think of how much water fills it. Measured in cubic units.

steps.t: Two types of Surface Area: LATERAL (or Curved) Surface Area = only the sides (not top/bottom). TOTAL Surface Area = all faces including top and bottom. CSA + top + bottom = TSA!
steps.s: Lateral or curved surface area is sides only. Total surface area includes all faces including top and bottom.

explain_questions.confirm_question: What is the unit of Volume?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: What is the unit of Volume?
nudges: Volume is 3-dimensional — it uses cubic units
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv1s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Surface Area = outer area (cm²) | Volume = inner space (cm³)</text></g><g id="sv1s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">SA: wrapping paper | V: water capacity</text></g><g id="sv1s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">LSA/CSA = sides only | TSA = CSA + top + bottom</text></g><g id="sv1ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Volume unit = cubic units (cm³ or m³)</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are the formulas for a Cuboid?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the formulas for a cuboid?

steps.t: A CUBOID has length l, breadth b, height h. It has 6 rectangular faces. LATERAL Surface Area = 2h(l+b) — only the 4 side faces. TOTAL Surface Area = 2(lb+bh+hl) — all 6 faces.
steps.s: Cuboid: length l, breadth b, height h. Lateral SA equals 2h times l plus b. Total SA equals 2 times lb plus bh plus hl.

steps.t: VOLUME of cuboid = l × b × h. Diagonal of cuboid = √(l²+b²+h²). Example: l=3, b=4, h=5. TSA = 2(12+20+15) = 2(47) = 94 cm². Volume = 60 cm³. Diagonal = √50 = 5√2 cm.
steps.s: Volume equals l times b times h. For 3, 4 and 5: total SA is 94, volume is 60, diagonal is 5 root 2.

steps.t: CUBE: special cuboid with l=b=h=a. TSA = 6a². LSA = 4a². Volume = a³. Diagonal = a√3. Memory trick: Total has 6 faces so 6a². Side has 4 faces so 4a²!
steps.s: Cube with side a: total SA equals 6a squared. Lateral SA equals 4a squared. Volume equals a cubed. Diagonal equals a root 3.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Total Surface Area of a cube with side 5 cm is ____.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Total Surface Area of a cube with side 5 cm is blank.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv2s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Cuboid: LSA=2h(l+b) | TSA=2(lb+bh+hl) | V=lbh</text></g><g id="sv2s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">l=3,b=4,h=5: TSA=94 | V=60 | diag=5√2</text></g><g id="sv2s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Cube side a: TSA=6a² | LSA=4a² | V=a³ | diag=a√3</text></g><g id="sv2ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">TSA = 6×25 = 150 cm²</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are the formulas for a Right Circular Cylinder?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the formulas for a right circular cylinder?

steps.t: A CYLINDER has radius r and height h. CURVED Surface Area (CSA) = 2πrh — just the curved side (like a tin can without top and bottom). TOTAL Surface Area (TSA) = 2πr(r+h) = CSA + 2πr² (two circular ends).
steps.s: Cylinder: radius r and height h. CSA equals 2 pi r h. TSA equals 2 pi r times r plus h.

steps.t: VOLUME of cylinder = πr²h — (area of base circle × height). Think: stack infinitely thin circles of area πr² up to height h! Example: r=7, h=10. CSA=2×22/7×7×10=440 cm². V=π×49×10=1540 cm³.
steps.s: Volume equals pi r squared h. For r equals 7 and h equals 10: CSA equals 440 and volume equals 1540 cubic cm.

steps.t: Value of π: use 22/7 when radius is a multiple of 7, or 3.14 otherwise. The CBSE exam always specifies which to use! Hollow cylinder has an inner and outer radius — find difference of TSAs.
steps.s: Use 22 over 7 when radius is multiple of 7. Use 3.14 otherwise. The exam specifies which to use.

explain_questions.confirm_question: CSA of a cylinder with radius 7 cm and height 5 cm is ____ (use π = 22/7).
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: CSA of a cylinder with radius 7 cm and height 5 cm, using pi equals 22 over 7.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv3s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Cylinder: CSA=2πrh | TSA=2πr(r+h) | V=πr²h</text></g><g id="sv3s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">r=7,h=10: CSA=440 | TSA=748 | V=1540 cm³</text></g><g id="sv3s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Use π=22/7 (r=multiple of 7) or π=3.14 (others)</text></g><g id="sv3ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">CSA = 2×(22/7)×7×5 = 220 cm²</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are the formulas for a Right Circular Cone?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the formulas for a right circular cone?

steps.t: A CONE has radius r, height h, and SLANT HEIGHT l = √(h²+r²). This is the key difference from cylinder — the cone has a slant height! CSA = πrl (just the curved surface). TSA = πr(r+l) = πrl + πr².
steps.s: Cone: radius r, height h, slant height l equals root of h squared plus r squared. CSA equals pi r l. TSA equals pi r times r plus l.

steps.t: VOLUME of cone = (1/3)πr²h. Exactly ONE-THIRD the volume of a cylinder with same radius and height! Beautiful relationship: 3 cones = 1 cylinder (same r and h).
steps.s: Volume of cone equals one third pi r squared h. Three cones equal one cylinder of same radius and height.

steps.t: Always find l first! Example: r=6, h=8. l=√(64+36)=√100=10 cm. CSA=π×6×10=60π≈188.4 cm². V=1/3×π×36×8=96π≈301.4 cm³. REMEMBER: find slant height before any cone formula!
steps.s: Always calculate slant height first. For r equals 6 and h equals 8: l equals 10. CSA equals 60 pi. Volume equals 96 pi.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Find the slant height of a cone with radius 5 cm and height 12 cm.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Find the slant height of a cone with radius 5 cm and height 12 cm.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv4s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Cone: l=√(h²+r²) | CSA=πrl | TSA=πr(r+l) | V=⅓πr²h</text></g><g id="sv4s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">r=6, h=8: l=10 | CSA=60π | V=96π</text></g><g id="sv4s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">3 cones = 1 cylinder (same r,h) | Find l FIRST!</text></g><g id="sv4ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">l = √(12²+5²) = √169 = 13 cm</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are the formulas for a Sphere and Hemisphere?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the formulas for a sphere and hemisphere?

steps.t: SPHERE (like a ball): radius r. Surface Area = 4πr² (only one surface — no flat faces!). Volume = (4/3)πr³. A sphere has NO flat face — only a curved surface, so SA = TSA = CSA = 4πr².
steps.s: Sphere with radius r: surface area equals 4 pi r squared. Volume equals 4 over 3 pi r cubed.

steps.t: HEMISPHERE (half sphere): Curved SA = 2πr². Total SA = 2πr² + πr² = 3πr² (add the flat circular base!). Volume = (2/3)πr³ (half of sphere volume).
steps.s: Hemisphere: curved SA equals 2 pi r squared. Total SA equals 3 pi r squared (add the flat base). Volume equals 2 over 3 pi r cubed.

steps.t: Memory trick: Sphere SA = 4πr² (4 circles worth of area). Volume = (4/3)πr³. Hemisphere: halve the volume → (2/3)πr³. Add circular base to get TSA: 3πr². The number 4, 3, 2 pattern helps!
steps.s: Pattern: sphere SA has coefficient 4. Volume has 4 over 3. Hemisphere volume has 2 over 3. TSA has coefficient 3.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Surface area of a sphere with radius 7 cm is ____ (use π = 22/7).
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Surface area of a sphere with radius 7 cm, using pi equals 22 over 7.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv5s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="190" height="56" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb"/><text x="105" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Sphere</text><text x="105" y="46" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#7a8c6e">SA=4πr² | V=4πr³/3</text></g><g id="sv5s1" opacity="0"><rect x="210" y="8" width="190" height="56" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="305" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Hemisphere</text><text x="305" y="46" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#b85c2a">TSA=3πr² | V=2πr³/3</text></g><g id="sv5s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Pattern: 4πr² → 3πr² | 4/3πr³ → 2/3πr³</text></g><g id="sv5ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">4×(22/7)×49 = 616 cm²</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you convert between units of volume?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you convert between units of volume?

steps.t: Key conversions: 1 cm³ = 1 mL (millilitre). 1 L = 1000 cm³ = 1000 mL. 1 m³ = 1,000,000 cm³ = 10,00,000 cm³ = 1000 litres. These appear in every CBSE problem!
steps.s: 1 cubic cm equals 1 millilitre. 1 litre equals 1000 cubic cm. 1 cubic metre equals 10 lakh cubic cm equals 1000 litres.

steps.t: Length: 1 m = 100 cm. Area: 1 m² = 10000 cm². Volume: 1 m³ = 10000×100 = 1000000 cm³. Each dimension is multiplied separately! Don't forget to cube the conversion factor for volume.
steps.s: 1 metre is 100 cm. 1 square metre is 10000 square cm. 1 cubic metre is 10 lakh cubic cm. Cube the factor for volume.

steps.t: Practical: A tank is 2m×3m×1.5m. Volume = 9 m³ = 9000 litres = 9,000,000 mL = 9,000,000 cm³. Always check: does the answer make physical sense? 9000 litres is about 9 large water drums!
steps.s: Tank 2 by 3 by 1.5 metres: volume is 9 cubic metres equals 9000 litres. Always check if answer is reasonable.

explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv6s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">1 cm³=1 mL | 1 L=1000 cm³ | 1 m³=10⁶ cm³=1000 L</text></g><g id="sv6s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Length×100 | Area×10000 | Volume×1000000</text></g><g id="sv6s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Tank 2×3×1.5m=9m³=9000L — check sense!</text></g><g id="sv6ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">1 litre = 1000 cm³</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you find the number of objects formed by melting a shape?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you find the number of objects formed by melting one shape?

steps.t: Key principle: when a shape is MELTED and recast into smaller shapes, the VOLUME is conserved (stays the same). Volume of original = Number of new objects × Volume of each new object.
steps.s: When melting and recasting, volume is conserved. Volume of original equals number of pieces times volume of each piece.

steps.t: Example: A sphere of radius 6 cm is melted and recast into small spheres of radius 1 cm. Number = Volume of big sphere / Volume of small sphere = (4/3)π(6³) / (4/3)π(1³) = 216/1 = 216 small spheres!
steps.s: Sphere radius 6 melted into spheres radius 1. Number equals 6 cubed divided by 1 cubed equals 216.

steps.t: The π and (4/3) cancel out! So it simplifies to just (6/1)³ = 216. In general: N = (R/r)³ for spheres. For cylinders: N = (R²H)/(r²h). Always set up: volume of big = N × volume of small.
steps.s: The common factors cancel. For spheres: N equals R over r, all cubed. Set up the equation and solve.

explain_questions.confirm_question: A sphere of radius 3 cm is melted into spheres of radius 1 cm. How many small spheres are formed?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: A sphere of radius 3 cm is melted into spheres of radius 1 cm. How many spheres are formed?
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv7s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Volume conserved: V_big = N × V_small</text></g><g id="sv7s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Sphere R=6 → spheres r=1: N=(4/3)π216/(4/3)π=216</text></g><g id="sv7s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Simplify: N=(R/r)³=(6/1)³=216 ✓</text></g><g id="sv7ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">N = (3/1)³ = 27 small spheres</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you solve problems involving water flowing through pipes?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you solve problems involving water flowing through pipes?

steps.t: Key idea: Volume of water flowing = Cross-section area of pipe × Length of water column. For a cylindrical pipe with radius r and water flowing at speed v m/s: Volume per second = πr²v.
steps.s: Water flowing through a pipe: volume per second equals cross section area times speed.

steps.t: Example: Pipe diameter = 14 cm (radius=7 cm). Water speed = 2 m/s = 200 cm/s. Volume per second = π × 49 × 200 = 9800π cm³/s ≈ 30,772 cm³/s ≈ 30.77 litres/s.
steps.s: Pipe radius 7 cm, speed 200 cm per second. Volume per second equals pi times 49 times 200 equals 9800 pi cubic cm per second.

steps.t: Time to fill a tank: Time = Volume of tank / Volume per second. Always convert units consistently (all in cm or all in m). Speed in m/s must match tank dimensions in m!
steps.s: Time to fill tank equals tank volume divided by flow rate. Always use consistent units throughout the calculation.

explain_questions.confirm_question: A cylindrical pipe has radius 1 cm and water speed 100 cm/s. Volume flowing per second = ____.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: A cylindrical pipe has radius 1 cm and water flows at 100 cm per second. Volume per second is blank.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv8s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Pipe flow: V/sec = πr² × speed</text></g><g id="sv8s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">r=7,speed=200: 9800π≈30.77 litres/s</text></g><g id="sv8s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Time = Tank volume / Flow rate | Use consistent units!</text></g><g id="sv8ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">V = π×1²×100 = 100π cm³/s</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the relationship between Cone, Cylinder and Sphere volumes?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the relationship between cone, cylinder and sphere volumes?

steps.t: For the SAME radius r and height h=2r (sphere diameter = cylinder height): Cone volume = (1/3)πr²(2r) = (2/3)πr³. Cylinder volume = πr²(2r) = 2πr³. Sphere volume = (4/3)πr³.
steps.s: For same radius and height equals 2r: cone volume is 2 over 3 pi r cubed, cylinder is 2 pi r cubed, sphere is 4 over 3 pi r cubed.

steps.t: Ratio: Cone : Sphere : Cylinder = (2/3)πr³ : (4/3)πr³ : 2πr³ = 1 : 2 : 3! Beautiful! One cone, one sphere, one cylinder (same r, h=2r) are in ratio 1:2:3.
steps.s: The ratio of cone to sphere to cylinder volumes is 1 to 2 to 3. This is a famous result by Archimedes.

steps.t: This amazing result was discovered by Archimedes! He was so proud that he asked for a diagram of a cylinder with an inscribed sphere to be carved on his tombstone. Archimedes' most favourite theorem!
steps.s: Archimedes discovered this 1 to 2 to 3 ratio and it was his proudest achievement. He wanted it on his tombstone.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Cone : Sphere : Cylinder volumes (same r, h=2r) are in ratio ____.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: What is the ratio of cone to sphere to cylinder volumes with same radius and height equals 2r?
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv9s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Same r, h=2r: Cone=2πr³/3 | Sphere=4πr³/3 | Cyl=2πr³</text></g><g id="sv9s1" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="46" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="66" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Cone : Sphere : Cylinder = 1 : 2 : 3</text></g><g id="sv9s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Archimedes loved this result so much → on his tombstone!</text></g><g id="sv9ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Cone : Sphere : Cylinder = 1 : 2 : 3</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you remember all the formulas?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you remember all the surface area and volume formulas?

steps.t: CUBOID: Think of a box. TSA=2(lb+bh+hl) — three pairs of faces. V=lbh. CUBE: all sides a. TSA=6a² (6 faces). V=a³. CYLINDER: CSA=2πrh (roll of paper), TSA=add two circles=2πr(r+h). V=πr²h.
steps.s: Box: TSA is 2 times lb plus bh plus hl. Cube: 6a squared and a cubed. Cylinder: roll it open to get CSA.

steps.t: CONE: l=√(h²+r²) first! CSA=πrl. TSA=πr(r+l). V=(1/3)πr²h. SPHERE: TSA=4πr². V=(4/3)πr³. HEMISPHERE: CSA=2πr², TSA=3πr². V=(2/3)πr³.
steps.s: Cone: always find slant height first. CSA equals pi rl. Sphere: 4 pi r squared and 4 over 3 pi r cubed. Hemisphere: halve the sphere.

steps.t: Quick memory table: Shape | CSA | TSA | Volume. Cube: 4a²,6a²,a³. Cylinder: 2πrh,2πr(r+h),πr²h. Cone: πrl,πr(r+l),(1/3)πr²h. Sphere: 4πr²,4πr²,(4/3)πr³. Hemisphere: 2πr²,3πr²,(2/3)πr³.
steps.s: Memorise the table. Practice each formula. The patterns make it easier: sphere is simpler than cylinder.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Volume of a cone is ____ × volume of cylinder with same radius and height.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Volume of a cone is blank times the volume of a cylinder with same radius and height.
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="sv10s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="24" font-size="10" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Cuboid:2(lb+bh+hl),lbh | Cube:6a²,a³ | Cyl:2πr(r+h),πr²h</text></g><g id="sv10s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="64" font-size="10" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Cone:πr(r+l),πr²h/3 (find l first!) | Sphere:4πr²,4πr³/3</text></g><g id="sv10s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="104" font-size="10" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Hemisphere:3πr²,2πr³/3 | Cone=⅓Cylinder (same r,h)</text></g><g id="sv10ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Cone = 1/3 × Cylinder volume</text></g>

practice_questions.question: Find the Total Surface Area of a cuboid with l=8cm, b=5cm, h=3cm.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the Total Surface Area of a cuboid with length 8, breadth 5 and height 3 cm.
practice_questions.trick: TSA of cuboid = 2(lb+bh+hl). Calculate each product: 8×5=40, 5×3=15, 3×8=24. Add: 79. Double: 158.
practice_questions.alt: Three pairs of faces: top+bottom=2×40=80, front+back=2×15=30, left+right=2×24=48. Total=80+30+48=158.

practice_questions.question: Find the volume of a cube with side 6 cm.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the volume of a cube with side 6 cm.
practice_questions.trick: Cube volume = side³. Just cube the side length! 6³=216. No formula needed beyond this!
practice_questions.alt: A cube is l×b×h but all sides are equal. So volume = 6×6×6 = 216 cm³.

practice_questions.question: Find the CSA of a cylinder with radius 14cm and height 10cm. (π=22/7)
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the curved surface area of a cylinder with radius 14 cm and height 10 cm, using pi equals 22 over 7.
practice_questions.trick: CSA=2πrh. With π=22/7 and r=14: the 7 cancels with 14 giving 2. So 2×22×2×10=880. Always look for cancellations!

practice_questions.question: Find the slant height of a cone with radius 9cm and height 12cm.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the slant height of a cone with radius 9 cm and height 12 cm.
practice_questions.trick: Always find l first for cone problems! 9-12-15 is a Pythagorean triple (3-4-5 tripled). Spot these: 5-12-13, 8-15-17, 9-12-15.

practice_questions.question: Find the volume of a cone with radius 7cm and height 6cm. (π=22/7)
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the volume of a cone with radius 7 cm and height 6 cm using pi equals 22 over 7.
practice_questions.trick: (1/3)×(22/7)×49×6. The 7 and 49=7² cancel to give 7. The 3 and 6 cancel to give 2. So 22×7×2=308.

practice_questions.question: Find the Surface Area of a sphere with radius 3.5cm. (π=22/7)
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the surface area of a sphere with radius 3.5 cm, using pi equals 22 over 7.

practice_questions.question: Volume of a hemisphere with radius 7cm is ____ (π=22/7).
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the volume of a hemisphere with radius 7 cm, using pi equals 22 over 7.
practice_questions.trick: (2/3)×(22/7)×7³=(2/3)×(22/7)×343=(2/3)×22×49=2156/3. Keep as fraction unless asked for decimal.
practice_questions.alt: (2/3)×(22/7)×343. The 7 in denominator and 343=7³ cancel to give 7²=49. (2/3)×22×49=2156/3.

practice_questions.question: A sphere of radius 4cm is melted to form small spheres of radius 1cm. How many small spheres?
practice_questions.question_spoken: A sphere of radius 4 cm is melted to form small spheres of radius 1 cm. How many small spheres are formed?
practice_questions.trick: N = (R/r)³ = (4/1)³ = 64. The π and (4/3) cancel! Just cube the ratio of radii.

practice_questions.question: TSA of a hemisphere with radius 10cm is ____ (π=3.14).
practice_questions.question_spoken: Total surface area of a hemisphere with radius 10 cm, using pi equals 3.14.
practice_questions.trick: Hemisphere TSA = 3πr². Curved part (2πr²) + flat circular base (πr²) = 3πr². Remember 3!

practice_questions.question: A cylinder has volume 1540 cm³ and radius 7cm. Find its height. (π=22/7)
practice_questions.question_spoken: A cylinder has volume 1540 cubic cm and radius 7 cm. Find its height.
practice_questions.trick: Volume given, find height: h = V/(πr²). Substitute and solve. (22/7)×49=22×7=154. Then 1540/154=10.

practice_questions.question: Find the volume of a cuboid with dimensions 12cm × 8cm × 5cm.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the volume of a cuboid with dimensions 12 cm by 8 cm by 5 cm.
practice_questions.trick: Volume of cuboid = l×b×h. Just multiply all three dimensions. 12×8=96, 96×5=480.
practice_questions.alt: Volume = length × breadth × height = 12×8×5 = 480 cubic cm.

practice_questions.question: The radius of a sphere is doubled. How many times does its volume increase?
practice_questions.question_spoken: If the radius of a sphere is doubled, how many times does its volume increase?
steps: New V = 8 × old V → Volume increases 8 times!
practice_questions.trick: Volume ∝ r³. Double r → volume multiplies by 2³=8. Triple r → 3³=27 times. The cube makes huge differences!
practice_questions.alt: Old V=(4/3)πr³. New V=(4/3)π(2r)³=(4/3)π×8r³=8×oldV. Eight times larger.

practice_questions.question: If the volume of a cube is 216 cm³, find its total surface area.
practice_questions.question_spoken: If the volume of a cube is 216 cubic cm, find its total surface area.
practice_questions.trick: Find side from volume: a=∛V. Then TSA=6a². For cube with V=216: a=6, TSA=6×36=216. Nice coincidence!

practice_questions.question: Ratio of volumes of a cone and cylinder with same radius and height is ____.
practice_questions.question_spoken: The ratio of volumes of a cone and cylinder with same radius and height is blank.
practice_questions.trick: Cone = (1/3) × Cylinder. So Cone:Cylinder = 1:3. Three cones exactly fill one cylinder!
practice_questions.alt: Divide both by πr²h: Cone=(1/3), Cylinder=1. Ratio=1/3:1=1:3.

practice_questions.question: Find the TSA of a cone with radius 6cm and slant height 10cm. (π=3.14)
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the total surface area of a cone with radius 6 cm and slant height 10 cm, using pi equals 3.14.
practice_questions.trick: TSA of cone = πr(r+l). Note: slant height l is already GIVEN here — no need to calculate it!
